industries.name: Steel & Cast Iron Alloying
uses: Molybdenum in low-alloy steels (0.1–0.3% Mo) dramatically improves hardenability, allowing thick sections to harden fully through their cross-section — essential for large-diameter oil country tubular goods (OCTG), pressure vessels, and heavy forgings

High-strength low-alloy steels for Arctic and sour-service oil pipelines require Mo additions for resistance to hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC) and sulfide stress corrosion cracking (SSCC) in H₂S-containing environments

Stainless steels: 316 and 316L (2% Mo), super-austenitic grades 904L and 254 SMO (4–6% Mo), and duplex grades 2205 and 2507 (3–4% Mo) — molybdenum improves resistance to pitting and crevice corrosion from chlorides in seawater and chemical plant environments

Tool steels: H13 (1.5% Mo), M2 high-speed steel (5% Mo, 6% W), D2 cold-work steel (1% Mo) — molybdenum increases red hardness (resistance to softening at elevated temperatures) in cutting tools, dies, and molds

Chromium-molybdenum (Cr-Mo) pressure vessel steels (ASTM P91, P92, P22): used for high-temperature steam pipes, headers, and pressure vessels in power plants — Mo stabilizes carbides and maintains creep strength at 550–650°C

companies.name: Freeport-McMoRan
companies.use: World's second-largest molybdenum producer. Recovers molybdenum as a byproduct of copper mining at Bingham Canyon (Kennecott), Morenci, and Climax (the world's first dedicated Mo mine). Produces molybdic oxide and ferromolybdenum.

companies.name: Codelco
companies.use: Major molybdenum producer as byproduct of copper mining at El Teniente (world's largest molybdenum mine by reserves), Chuquicamata, and Andina operations. Chile is the world's largest molybdenum producer country.

companies.name: China Molybdenum (CMOC)
companies.use: Major Chinese molybdenum producer from the Luanchuan molybdenum deposit in Henan — one of the world's largest primary Mo mines. China produces ~40% of world molybdenum. CMOC also operates major copper-cobalt mines in the DRC.

companies.name: Climax Molybdenum (Freeport)
companies.use: Operates the Climax mine in Colorado and Henderson mine in Colorado — both dedicated molybdenum deposits (not copper byproduct). Climax was the world's largest molybdenum mine for much of the 20th century.

industries.name: Petroleum Refining Catalysts
uses: Cobalt-molybdenum (CoMo) and nickel-molybdenum (NiMo) hydrodesulfurization (HDS) catalysts in petroleum refineries remove sulfur from gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel to meet ultra-low-sulfur fuel standards (10 ppm in EU and US)

CoMo and NiMo catalysts supported on alumina (Al₂O₃) operate at 300–400°C and 30–130 bar hydrogen pressure in fixed-bed reactors — the largest installed base of molybdenum-containing catalysts in the world by total metal inventory
Hydrodenitrogenation (HDN) and hydrocracking catalysts also use NiMo formulations to remove nitrogen and crack heavy molecules in vacuum gas oil upgrading
Molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) as active phase in HDS catalysts — MoS₂ edges are the catalytic sites; CoMo or NiMo promoters increase activity by orders of magnitude
Spent HDS catalyst recycling recovers molybdenum and cobalt/nickel — a significant secondary supply source for molybdenum chemicals and metal

companies.name: Haldor Topsoe (Topsoe)
companies.use: World's leading HDS catalyst supplier. Produces CoMo (TK-series) and NiMo catalysts for ultra-deep desulfurization of diesel and gasoline. Supplies to refineries globally for Euro 6 / Tier 3 ultra-low-sulfur fuel production.

companies.name: BASF Catalysts
companies.use: Major refinery catalyst producer making CoMo and NiMo HDS, HDN, and hydrocracking catalysts for petroleum refineries worldwide. Also recovers molybdenum from spent catalyst through its catalyst recycling operations.

companies.name: Albemarle Corporation
companies.use: Major refinery and petrochemical catalyst producer. Manufactures CoMo and NiMo HDS catalysts for diesel desulfurization and provides catalyst recycling services recovering molybdenum and other metals from spent catalysts.

industries.name: Lubricants & Specialty Applications
uses: Molybdenum disulfide (MoS₂) dry lubricant: lamellar crystal structure allows easy shear between layers — used as grease additive, dry film lubricant spray, and burnished coating for aerospace fasteners, gun mechanisms, and precision bearings in vacuum

Molybdenum dithiocarbamate (MoDTC) friction modifier in engine oil — reduces boundary friction in cam-follower contacts by 20–30%, improving fuel economy in automotive engines; required in ILSAC GF-6 engine oil specification
MoS₂ coating on titanium implant screws and surgical instruments reduces galling during installation and removal in orthopedic and spinal surgery
Molybdenum trioxide (MoO₃) as smoke suppressant and flame retardant synergist in PVC wire insulation and other polymers — reduces acidity and smoke opacity in fire conditions
Molybdate corrosion inhibitors in cooling water systems and automotive antifreeze — sodium molybdate (Na₂MoO₄) passivates steel and aluminum surfaces at low concentrations (50–200 ppm)

companies.name: Dow Corning / Dow
companies.use: Produces molybdenum disulfide-containing lubricant greases and dry film lubricant sprays for aerospace, defense, and industrial applications under the Molykote brand — the benchmark MoS₂ lubricant product globally.

companies.name: Afton Chemical (NewMarket)
companies.use: Major engine oil additive producer. Supplies MoDTC (molybdenum dithiocarbamate) friction modifiers to engine oil blenders for passenger car motor oils meeting ILSAC GF-5 and GF-6 fuel economy specifications.
